coredata.dc:title: The optical fiber sensing platform for ferric ions detection: A practical application for carbon quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Cai, Yi
Zhang, Jing
Zhang, Min
Wang, Minghao
Zhao, Yong
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as one kind of promising carbon nanomaterials, are widely used as fluorescent probes for metal ions sensing. An optical fiber sensing platform is established as a significant application of the CQDs to realize online detection in narrow space. The assembling process of the sensing probe can be accomplished by the method of in-situ polymerization without any mold in seconds. The maximum diameter of the designed sensing probe is only 220 µm. The sensing system is quite light that the total weight of the system with a 10 m-long optical fiber is only 3.2 kg, showing great potential application in field. Here, ferric ion is set as an example to test the performance of the sensing platform. The ferric ions in the concentration range of 0–250 μM can be quantitatively analysis by the proposed platform along with a detection limit of 0.6 μM, which is comparable with the method of dissolving the same concentration of the CQDs in the aqueous solution. The response time of the sensing system is only 6 s. The sensing platform provides real-time fluorescence variation. The practicability of the present platform is validated by Fe3+ detection in certified reference materials and real-world water samples. The sensing platform is one of the novel and promising applications of carbon-based materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Optical fiber
Carbon quantum dots
Fluorescence enhancement
Sensing platform
Hydrogel film
Fe3+ real-time detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The fabrication of the sensing probe
Ferric ions sensing in real time

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Science Foundation for Distinguished Young Scholars of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: China National Funds for Distinguished Young Scientists

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Science Foundation of Hebei Province
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Hebei Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21904017 ), Natural Science Foundation of Hebei Province ( B2019501073 , F2020501040 ).

Yong Zhao received MA and PhD degrees, respectively, from Harbin Institute of Technology in 1998 and 2001. Then he worked as a postdoctoral research fellow, an associate professor until 2009 in Tsinghua University. Now he is working at the Northeastern University as a full professor. In 2014, he was awarded by the National Science Foundation for Distinguished Young Scholars of China. In 2015, he was honored as the Yangtze River Scholar Distinguished Professor by the Ministry of Education of China. His current research interests are the development of fiber-optic sensors and device, fiber Bragg grating sensors, novel sensor materials and principles, and optical measurement technologies. He has authored and co-authored more than 300 scientific papers, 60 patents, and 5 books.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic diagram of the sensing probe assembling process.
Schematic diagram of the optical fiber sensing platform for ferric ions detection. The inset is the photograph of the sensing probe coated by the CQDs.

(a) TEM image of the CQDs; (b) XPS spectrum of the CQDs; (c) and (d) are the high-resolution XPS spectrum of the C 1 s and N 1 s of the CQDs, respectively; (e) UV–vis absorption spectrum (red line) and FL spectra (black line) of the CQDs solution, insert: photographs of the CQDs solution under daylight (left) and UV light (right); (f) the fluorescence emission spectra of the CQDs solution with excitation wavelengths from 320 nm to 400 nm.
Optical simulation diagrams of the light transmission out of the optical fiber tips; (a) the flat optical fiber tip; (b) the designed optical fiber tip.

(a) - (e) The hydrogel polymerization on the spherical tip under 0–15 s UV irradiation; (f) The normalized FL intensity collected by different sensing probes, inset: the microphotographs of the sensing probes in different shapes.

(a)The fluorescence spectra of different Fe3+ concentrations obtained by the present sensing system; (b) The corresponding calibration curves; (c) The response time of the sensing probe in 20 μM Fe3+ solution (pH 7.4).

(a) The relative fluorescence of the sensing platform induced by 200 μM Fe3+ and 10 mM Cr3+, Co2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Al3+ and Mn2+, respectively, inset: the photograph of fluorescence emissions of hydrogel doped with 6.55 × 10−3% (w/v) CQDs with 200 μM different metal ions excited by 365 nm UV light; (b) Dependence of the relative fluorescence of the sensing probe on pH values.

(a) The relative fluorescence induced by 20 μM Fe3+ with and without 200 μM various compounds, respectively; (b) The relative fluorescence induced by 200 μM Fe3+ with and without 200 μM various compounds, respectively (mixed solution: 200 μM Cr3+, Ca2+, Co2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Al3+, Mn2+, K+ and Na+, and 200 μM citric acid, urea, L-glutamic acid, L-serine and L-lysine).
Determination of ferric ions in certified reference material and real-world samples by employing the present sensing platform (n = 3).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The certified solution was diluted 100 times before determination;

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The certified solution was diluted 10 times before determination.

head.ce:title: The optical fiber sensing platform for ferric ions detection: A practical application for carbon quantum dots

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Control Engineering, Hebei Key Laboratory of Micro-Nano Precision Optical Sensing and Measurement Technology, Northeastern University at Qinhuangdao

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as one kind of promising carbon nanomaterials, are widely used as fluorescent probes for metal ions sensing. An optical fiber sensing platform is established as a significant application of the CQDs to realize online detection in narrow space. The assembling process of the sensing probe can be accomplished by the method of in-situ polymerization without any mold in seconds. The maximum diameter of the designed sensing probe is only 220 µm. The sensing system is quite light that the total weight of the system with a 10 m-long optical fiber is only 3.2 kg, showing great potential application in field. Here, ferric ion is set as an example to test the performance of the sensing platform. The ferric ions in the concentration range of 0–250 μM can be quantitatively analysis by the proposed platform along with a detection limit of 0.6 μM, which is comparable with the method of dissolving the same concentration of the CQDs in the aqueous solution. The response time of the sensing system is only 6 s. The sensing platform provides real-time fluorescence variation. The practicability of the present platform is validated by Fe3+ detection in certified reference materials and real-world water samples. The sensing platform is one of the novel and promising applications of carbon-based materials.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, carbon nanomaterials attracted widespread attentions all over the world because of their excellent optical, electronic and biocompatible properties [1]. The carbon nanomaterials can serve as fluorescent probes for biosensing and bioimaging, which provide new routes and opportunities in the fields of food, drug loading, chemical industry, environmental protection and so on [2,3]. Among these carbon nanomaterials, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are regarded as one of the most promising fluorescent materials. The biocompatible CQDs have broad application in biosensing, bioimaging, optoelectronics, photocatalysis, etc [4–6]. The precursor reagents are available and the synthesis methods are usually simple. Moreover, the surface of the CQDs can be designed easily to tune the optical properties. In the previous researches, some typical metal ions, including Ag+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Pb2+, etc, have been selectively recognized and detected by CQDs fluorescent probes [7–9]. However, the water-soluble CQDs probes need to be dissolved to the aqueous samples during the analysis process. It is challenging to accomplish real-time and online detection because of the limitation of laser delivery and fluorescence collection in aqueous environments [10].

Optical fiber sensor could accomplish real-time monitoring of heavy metal ions with the specific advantages of small size, antielectromagnetic interference, high sensitivity and chemical inertness [11–13]. Some typical fiber gratings are employed for metal ions online detection. Although the fiber sensors based on Bragg grating (FBG) with flexible structures have low insertion losses and their spectra are easy to demodulate, the mechanical strength of the FBG-based sensors are usually weak. Their structures are often prepared by corrosion method [14]. For the long period fiber gratings (LPFG)-based sensors, the mechanical strength of the sensors is stronger than FBG-based sensors, but they are hard to excise the spectral interferences between the core modes and cladding modes [15,16]. The fiber sensors based on surface plasmon resonance (SPR) and localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) can be also applied to heavy metal ions sensing. However, the thickness and uniformity of the noble metal film on the surface of the optical fiber affect the sensing characteristics a lot. The SPR- and LSPR-based sensors require high coating techniques [17–19].

The fluorescence-based sensors for heavy metal ions detection are reliable, sensitive, and with high selectivity [20]. The fluorescence characteristics (the intensity/wavelength of fluorescence spectrum, or the fluorescence lifetime) of the functionalized molecules on the fiber probe vary with the species and/or the concentrations of target analytes. In the reported works, the fluorescence-based fiber sensors usually consist of the excitation light source, the sensing probe and the detector [20]. The fluorescent material, such as the labeled protein and nucleic acids, semiconductor-type quantum dots and carbon dots, are immobilized on the sensing probe [21–24]. Among these functional fluorescent materials, carbon dots show great potential for metal ions detection. In 2012, an optical fiber sensor for Hg2+ based on carbon nanoparticles was reported [25]. The carbon dots were immobilized on the tapered fiber probe of 600 µm diameter with layer-by-layer technique. The fluorescence of the immobilized carbon dots was quenched by the mercury ions. Another fluorescent hydrogel waveguide was fabricated by for Hg2+ online sensing [10]. The biocompatible hydrogels could be employed in tissue engineering and drug delivery. The slab waveguide (40 mm length × 5 mm width × 0.55–1.1 mm thickness) was made by a certain concentration CDs and 40% (w/v) PEGDA hydrogels with the custom mold under UV irradiation in a few minutes. The optical fiber was cleaved at 12° to couple with the slab waveguide. The CDs-based fluorescent sensors should not only present excellent analytical performances for metal ions sensing, but also possess the potential for practical applications. In this respect, the CDs-based fluorescent sensors would present more advantages in applications for different situations.

In this work, a fiber sensing platform based on carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are developed for measurement of metal ions concentration. The designed sensing probe is only 220 µm, which is much thinner than the reported fluorescent fiber sensors, can be applied in narrow space. Although the maximum diameter of the fiber tip is small, the CQDs on the fiber tip can be effectively excited. Besides, the assembling of the sensing probe can be accomplished in seconds without molds. Here, the detection of ferric ions is set an example to illustrate the practicality and validity of the proposed fiber sensing platform. The carbon quantum dots are synthesized for ferric ions sensing by DL-malic acid and ethanolamine. The synthesis route and the sensing process corresponding to ferric ions are shown in Fig. S1. The obtained CQDs is coated on the fiber tip. When ferric ions are added into the aqueous solution, the fluorescence of the sensing probe enhances. The sensing platform provides comparable limit of detection with the method of dissolving same concentration of the CQDs in the aqueous solution. The ferric ions in the concentration range of 0–250 μM can be quantitatively analysis by the platform along with a detection limit of 0.6 μM. The practicability of the present sensing platform is validated by ferric ions determination in real samples.

The carbon quantum dots were synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method. 1.0 g DL-malic acid and 300 μL ethanolamine were dissolved in 30 mL H2O. And then, the homogeneous solution was transferred to a 50 mL teflon-lined hydrothermal reactor for heating. After 8 h heating at 180 ℃, the yellow liquid was obtained. The liquid was purified in ultrapure water with the dialysis membrane (molecular weight cut-off 500) for 72 h. The purified solution was heated at 60 ℃ overnight, and then the CQDs powder is obtained. The further characterization information of the CQDs can be acquired by the analytical methods of the transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and X-Ray Diffraction (XRD). The results are illustrated and discussed in “3.1. Characterizations of the CQDs”.

The spherical tip (diameter 200 µm) is prepared on the end of the multimode fiber (core/cladding diameter: 105/125 µm) with a fusion splicer. The specific process and the operation parameter of the fusion splicer are described in the Supplementary Materials (Preparation of the spherical optical fiber tip), and the illustration is shown in Fig. S2.

The spherical fiber tip was inserted into the hydrogel precursor solution to assemble the sensing tip. The other end of the fiber was connected to the UV light source (M365FP1, Thorlabs, USA) with 0.15 A working current. Some investigations are launched to select the concentration of PEGDA, photoinitiator and CQDs for hydrogel film polymerization. The results are shown in Fig. S3. According to the results, 6.55 × 10−3% (w/v) CQDs are dissolved in the hydrogel precursor solution with 38% (w/v) PEGDA (700 Da) and 1.8% (w/v) 2-hydroxy-2-methyl-propiophenone (photoinitiator). The UV light transmitted to the spherical tip and induced hydrogel polymerization on the surface of the tip. The assembling process of the sensing probe is shown in Fig. 1.

The present sensing platform, which is displayed in Fig. 2, consists of a laser source, an optical sensing unit and a detector. The multimode fiber with the diameter of 105/125 µm (core/cladding) is used through the sensing platform. A 375 nm laser (Fiblaser Technology Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China) is employed as the light source. The prepared sensing probe is connected to the end of a 50:50 fiber coupler to compose the optical sensing unit. There is a long-pass filter (<400 nm, Thorlabs, USA) connected between the sensing unit and the detector. The extra light could be filtered in this way for background correction. The detector is a spectrometer (Ocean Optics PRO 2000, USA) with a laptop. The total weight of the above-mentioned platform with 10 m-long optical fiber is only about 3.2 kg (including a 1.4 kg laptop).
The descriptions of the chemical reagents and other apparatus are stated in detail in the Supplementary Materials (Chemicals and apparatus).

In this section, the characterization of the CQDs, fabrication of the sensing probe, ferric ions sensing in real time, interferences and application for real-world samples of the proposed sensing system are presented in both tabular and graphic forms.

The CQDs are the key part of the sensitive film of the sensing probe. Here, the CQDs were synthesized by carbonaceous and nitrogenous compounds by one-pot hydrothermal method. The TEM image of the CQDs in Fig. 3 (a) shows that the CQDs are nearly spherical particles at nanoscale. The statistics on sizes of the particles are in the range of 1.0–5.0 nm. The chemical element composition is investigated by analyzing XPS. As shown in Fig. 3 (b), three peaks are found at 294.85, 400.12 and 532.38 eV, which are respectively attributed to C 1s, N 1s and O 1s. The C 1s spectrum can be resolved as C-C (284.76 eV), C–N (285.73 eV), C–O (286.52 eV) and CO (288.8 eV) respectively, as shown in Fig. 3 (c) [26–28]. The N 1s spectrum in Fig. 3 (d) has two peaks located at 400.09 eV and 401.69 eV, which are attributed to C–N and N–H [27,29]. The peaks of C–O and CO can be found in the O 1s spectrum (shown in Fig. S4). The FTIR spectrum of the CQDs (Fig. S5) demonstrates the existence of the above groups which are mentioned in the XPS results. The details of the FTIR results are described in Supplementary Materials. As shown in XRD pattern (Fig. S6), one evident peak at 19.79° demonstrates carbon exists in amorphous state [29].

The optical properties of the CQDs solution are also investigated. The ultraviolet-visible absorption spectrum and fluorescence spectrum of the CQDs are shown in Fig. 3 (e). The inset in Fig. 3 (e) is the photograph of the CQDs solution under daylight (left) and UV light (right) respectively. The emission spectra caused by UV light ranged from 320 nm to 400 nm are shown in Fig. 3 (f). With the excitation wavelength range from 340 nm to 380 nm, the high emission efficiency can be achieved. It is appropriate to choose a 375 nm laser as UV light source in the sensing platform.

As the results of the above characterization analysis, there are abundant oxygen-, carbon-, and nitrogen-containing functional groups on the surface of the CQDs. With a concentration of 6.55 × 10−3% (w/v) CQDs in PBS (pH 7.4), the fluorescence intensity on Fe3+ concentration in the range of 0–250 μM are recorded and the corresponding standard curve is shown in Fig. S7. The limit of detection (LOD) obtained by the method of dissolving the CQDs in aqueous solution is 0.4 μM. The CQDs in solution show satisfactory performance on Fe3+ detection. The CQDs are employed for subsequent hydrogel film coating.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The fabrication of the sensing probe
ce:section.ce:para: In the proposed system, the multimode fiber with small diameter is employed through the experimental platform. In this way, the cost can be reduced greatly for remote monitoring. Besides, it offers the opportunity for the sensing in narrow space.

Here, the end of the fiber tip is designed spherical according to the result of optical simulation. The optical simulation diagrams of the light transmission in the flat optical fiber tip and the spherical optical fiber tip are shown in Fig. 4. The diameter of the optical fiber is set as 105/125 µm (core/cladding) in the simulation. The diameter of the spherical tip is 200 µm in the optical simulation, which is consistent with the fiber tip used in the experimental platform. The simulations demonstrate that the light does not travel straightly after passing through the designed fiber tip. With the spherical tip, the light is divorced from center point of the sphere. The designed fiber tip under microscope is shown in Fig. 5 (a). When the fiber tip inserts into the hydrogel precursor solution, the PEGDA hydrogel film can be in-situ polymerized under UV irradiation on the fiber tip following the direction of light propagation. The shape of the sensitive film depends on the UV light route through the fiber tip. Fig. 5(b) to (e) displays the in-situ polymerization of hydrogel film on the designed fiber tip under 2 s, 7 s, 12 s and 15 s UV irradiation, respectively. The result shows that the polymerization of the hydrogel film on the surface of the tip accords the result of optical simulation. The sizes of the sensing probe under different UV irradiation are marked in Fig. 5. It can be noticed that the maximum diameter of the sensing probe is limited in about 220 µm within 12 s. The design of the spherical tip limits the lateral overgrowth of the hydrogels. After 12 s irradiation, the film grows laterally rapidly. The maximum diameter of the sensing probe reaches 500 µm under 15 s irradiation, as shown in Fig. 5 (e). The fluorescence spectra collected by sensing probes with different polymerization time are shown in Fig. 5 (f). It can be seen that the fluorescence intensity increases with the size of the sensing probe. Although the stronger fluorescence can be collected by the larger sensing probe, the probe with 12 s UV irradiation is adopted in the platform for further measurement in narrow space. The advantage of the proposed coating method is obvious. It takes much shorter compared with layer-by-layer self-assembly methods [25]. Besides, the proposed method simplifies the coating process greatly compared with previous researches [10,30–32]. There is no custom mold used in this work.

Usually, the pore size of PEGDA hydrogels (Mr 575–20000) is in the range of less than 0.1–10 nm, while the size of the CQDs is in the range of 1–5 nm, which is shown in Fig. 3 (a). In the CQDs doped hydrogel, the CQDs can be trapped in the PEGDA polymer network [10]. In our experiment, from the desorption data of Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) analysis, the results can be calculated that the BET surface area is 11.4 m2 g−1 and the pore size distribution (PSD) according to the Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) method is about 5 nm. The result of BET analysis demonstrates the existence of network structure in the hydrogel film.

Some experiments were conducted to test the stability of the proposed sensing probe. The sensing probe was immersed into DI water with 167 Hz vibration. It shows that the fluorescence spectrum remained constant after 30 min maniacal vibration. Besides, the fluorescence intensity of the sensing probe exhibits stable in 4 days, which shows no leakage of the CQDs from the PEGDA hydrogels on the fiber tip. The fluorescence spectra before and after 30 min maniacal vibration and the long-term stability of fluorescence intensity in DI water are shown in Fig. S8.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ferric ions sensing in real time
ce:section.ce:para: The sensing probe is spliced to the platform to develop a complete and consistent system for real-time and remote sensing. The fluorescence of the CQDs in PEGDA hydrogels has a redshift compared with the CQDs in DI water. The results in Fig. 6 (a) show the fluorescence intensity of the sensing probe increases with Fe3+ concentration. Compared with Fig. S7, the trend of the curve is similar to the curve of the detection method by dissolving the CQDs in aqueous solution.

As shown in Fig. 6 (b), the sensing platform can be used for quantitative detection of ferric ions in the range of 0–250 μM. The regression equations of the calibration curves are (I- I0)/I0 = 0.00871c + 0.0655 (R2 = 0.9788) and (I- I0)/I0 = 0.000929c+ 0.277 (R2 = 0.9667), for the range of 0–25 μM and 25–250 μM, respectively. It can be concluded that at low concentration of Fe3+, the functional groups on the surface of CQDs have a large formation constant (Kf) with Fe3+, and by increasing the concentration of Fe3+, the Kf becomes small. This makes the calibration curve changed when the Fe3+ concentration is 25 μM. The LOD of the proposed platform for Fe3+ is 0.6 μM. The LOD of the present sensing platform is comparable with the LOD obtained by dissolving the same concentration of CQDs in solution (0.4 μM). The LOD of the proposed platform is satisfactory although optical fiber with small diameter is employed. Meanwhile, the platform is available to deliver and collect the fluorescence variation online.

The response time of the sensing probe was measured to validate the real-time sensing. Fig. 6 (c) records the fluorescence intensity of the sensing probe at 531 nm right after immersing the probe in 20 μM Fe3+ solution (pH 7.4). The fluorescence increases rapidly and reaches nearly equilibrium in only about 6 s. The rapid response is owing to the porous structure of the hydrogel polymer and appropriate thickness of the hydrogel film on the sensing probe.

The enhancement mechanism on addition of ferric ions may attribute to photo-induced electron transfer “OFF” phenomenon [33]. As the inactive photophysically, Fe3+ cannot induce electron or energy transfer of receptor molecules. The electron-donating ability of the lone pairs on the surface of the CQDs is suppressed [34]. This may cause the difference in selectivity of ferric ions and other ions. Interference experiment is conducted to confirm the selectively detection of ferric ions by the present sensing platform. Some different metal ions with the concentration of 200 μM, including Fe3+, Cr3+, Co2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Al3+ and Mn2+, were added to the hydrogel precursor solution with 6.55 × 10−3% (w/v) CQDs. After a few seconds UV irradiation, the fluorescence of the hydrogel polymers with different metal ions are shown in the inset of Fig. 7 (a). Learning from the photograph, the fluorescence of the hydrogel doped with CQDs is enhanced by Fe3+ obviously, while the fluorescence of the hydrogel doped with other metal ions has no change compared with blank identified by naked eye. When the sensing probe was immersed into the solutions containing 200 μM Fe3+ and 10 mM other metal ions (Cr3+, Co2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Al3+ and Mn2+) respectively, the relative fluorescence intensity changed by various metal ions. The results are displayed in Fig. 7 (a).

Among these metal ions, only ferric ions enhance the fluorescence obviously. The fluorescence lifetimes of CQDs before and after 20 μM Fe3+ addition display in Table S1. The prolonged fluorescence lifetime after Fe3+ addition may be attributed to the suppression of intramolecular vibration by CQDs-Fe3+ aggregate, leading to suppression of nonradiative transitions and radiation decay [35,36]. As shown in Fig. 7 (b), the relative fluorescence of the sensing probe exhibits nearly stable in the pH range of 4–9, which is conducive to the applications of the proposed platform.

The selectivity of the sensing system is validated further. The results are shown in Fig. 8. Some compounds are added to 20 μM and 200 μM Fe3+ solution, which are in the two slopes of the calibration curve, respectively. It is shown that when various compounds (including 200 μM Cr3+, Ca2+, Co2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Al3+, Mn2+, K+ and Na+, and 200 μM citric acid, urea, L-glutamic acid, L-serine and L-lysine) are added to the blank, the fluorescence remains nearly constant, while when some Fe3+ (20 μM or 200 μM) are added to the mixed solution, the fluorescence enhanced obviously. Besides, the fluorescence intensity of the mixed solution with 20 μM/200 μM Fe3+ is approximately equal to the solution contains only 20 μM/200 μM Fe3+. In addition, the fluorescence intensity obtained from 20 μM/200 μM Fe3+ solution with 200 μM Cr3+, Ca2+, Co2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Al3+, Mn2+, K+ and Na+, and 200 μM citric acid, urea, L-glutamic acid, L-serine and L-lysine are also displayed in Fig. 8. It turns out that all these compounds have little interference on Fe3+ sensing. The interference experiment demonstrates that the fluorescence is not influenced by the sample matrix easily, and the sensing platform would make application in real conditions.

The present sensing system is used to determination ferric ions in real-world samples, including certified reference materials of GNM-M041596–2013 (water, from National Center for Standard Materials, China), tap water (Qinhuangdao, China) and rainwater (Qinhuangdao, China). The detection results are given in Table 1. It is obvious that reasonable agreements are obtained between the certified and the found values for the standard sample. And also, the recoveries obtained from the tap water and rainwater are 103% and 102% respectively. The reliability and the practicability are validated.

To sum up, the design and the sensing mechanism of the sensing platform is discussed. The fiber probe can be assembled by the method of in-situ polymerization in seconds. The maximum diameter of the probe is only 220 µm thus the sensing probe has great potential application in the narrow space. The rapid response of the platform for ferric ions has also been proved in the experiment. The internal and external inferences of the aqueous environment for the present platform, e.g., some coexisting compounds (other metal ions, amino acids, etc.), pH value, temperature and vibration, has been investigated in this work.

In this work, an optical fiber sensing platform for online and real-time Fe3+ detection is developed. The detection limit of the sensing platform is proved comparable with the method of dissolving the same concentration of CQDs in the aqueous solution. The limitation of laser delivery and emission collection in aqueous environments has been broken through by the present platform. The proposed fluorescent sensing platform shows high sensitivity, high selectivity and rapid response to Fe3+ sensing. The sensing probe is designed for narrow space. The CQDs in sensitive hydrogel film can be replaced for expanded demands. The in-situ polymerization on the fiber tip may inspire various methods for design and fabrication of sensing probe based on the optical simulation. Besides, by virtue of its small size, the sensing platform can be applied for metal ions analysis in emergency.

Yi Cai: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Jing Zhang: Investigation, Validation. Min Zhang: Methodology. Minghao Wang: Investigation. Yong Zhao: Supervision & Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21904017), Natural Science Foundation of Hebei Province (B2019501073, F2020501040).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2022.131857.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material.
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon nanotubes: present and future commercial applications
Science

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.F.L. De Volder, S.H. Tawfick, R.H. Baughman, A.J. Hart, Carbon nanotubes: present and future commercial applications, Science 339 (2013) 535–539. https://doi.org/10.1126/science.1222453.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent applications of carbon nanomaterials in fluorescence biosensing and bioimaging
Chem. Commun.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Wen, Y. Xu, H. Li, A. Lu, S. Sun, Recent applications of carbon nanomaterials in fluorescence biosensing and bioimaging, Chem. Commun. 51 (2015) 11346–11358. https://doi.org/10.1039/c5cc02887f.

Lett. 30 (2019) 1410–1414. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cclet.2019.04.011.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yi Cai was born in Liaoning Province, China in 1989. She received her Ph.D. degree in Analytical Chemistry (2018) from Northeastern University, China. In 2018, she joined Northeastern University (Qinhuangdao Campus) as a lecturer. Her current research is focus on the development of fiber sensing platforms based on novel fluorescent materials and the development of other miniaturized photoelectric devices for detection.

Jing Zhang was born in Hebei Province, China, in December 1996. She is currently pursuing the M.A. degree in Control Engineering with the School of Controlling Engineering, Northeastern University (Qinhuangdao Campus), China. Her supervisor is Dr. Y. Cai. Her research interest is optical fiber sensing system for the detection of metal ions based on fluorescent carbon dots.
Min Zhang is an associate professor in School of Engineering, Shenyang Agricultural University. His current research is focus on photoelectric detection technology.

Minghao Wang received the M. A. degrees from Northeastern University (Qinhuangdao Campus). His supervisor is Prof. Y. Zhao. His research is focused on optical fiber sensors, carbon nanomaterials, and measurements and systems.

Yong Zhao received MA and PhD degrees, respectively, from Harbin Institute of Technology in 1998 and 2001. Then he worked as a postdoctoral research fellow, an associate professor until 2009 in Tsinghua University. Now he is working at the Northeastern University as a full professor. In 2014, he was awarded by the National Science Foundation for Distinguished Young Scholars of China. In 2015, he was honored as the Yangtze River Scholar Distinguished Professor by the Ministry of Education of China. His current research interests are the development of fiber-optic sensors and device, fiber Bragg grating sensors, novel sensor materials and principles, and optical measurement technologies. He has authored and co-authored more than 300 scientific papers, 60 patents, and 5 books.
